---
title: "Cabbage Palmetto (Sabal palmetto)"
slug: "sabal-palmetto"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622625640/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Cabbage Palmetto"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Arecales"
tax_family: "Arecaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Arecales", "Arecaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622625640/medium.jpg" alt="Cabbage Palmetto" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/stevecollins">
stevecollins</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341959597">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Arecales
-   **Family:** Arecaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=123481)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in a wide range of habitats including coastal areas, hammocks, wetlands, and urban landscapes.

## Natural history notes

A large palm characterized by its unbranched trunk and fan-shaped leaves. In Florida, it is found in a wide range of habitats including coastal areas, hammocks, wetlands, and urban landscapes. It grows year-round in warm conditions and produces small flowers followed by dark fruits that provide food for wildlife, while also serving as an iconic and resilient component of Florida’s native flora.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/123481-Sabal-palmetto)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/639)
